chunk_summary: This section outlines discharge nutrition prescriptions and follow-up endpoints for patients with chronic cardiorespiratory disease, emphasizing precise instructions, growth targets, recovery methods, and follow-up schedules tailored to risk levels.
learning_objectives: Understand the essential components of a discharge nutrition prescription for chronic cardiorespiratory disease patients.
Learn how to set growth endpoints using weight velocity or z-score trajectories.
Identify safe recovery methods for missed feeds and illness plans for hydration and calorie maintenance.
Recognize appropriate follow-up intervals based on patient risk categories.
key_facts: Discharge nutrition prescriptions must include route plan, density, schedule, and safety stop rules.
Target energy and protein: kcal/kg/day and g/kg/day, with growth endpoints like weight velocity or z-score trajectory.
Follow-up for high-risk groups: within 7–14 days post-discharge; standard-risk: every 2–4 weeks until stable.
Missed-feed recovery methods include small extra boluses or nocturnal catch-up, avoiding recovery in cases of aspiration risk or heart failure volume limits.
Escalate follow-up if intake <75% of target for >48–72 hours or if z-scores drop by ≥0.5–1 over a short interval.
reference_quote: A discharge nutrition prescription should read like a medication order: specific enough that another caregiver can reproduce it without interpretation. (Source: https://www.bapen.org.uk/pdfs/enteral-feed-monitoring.pdf)
document_key_topics: Follow-up schedules for high-risk and standard-risk patients
